questions.q: Which ocean is the largest by surface area and volume?
Which region is known for having the highest tidal ranges in the world?
What type of microscopic organisms produce roughly half of Earth's oxygen?
What name is given to the boundary between warm surface water and cold deep ocean water?
Which marine organisms are famous for building coral reefs?
Which type of ocean current is driven primarily by wind on the surface?
What is the term for the ocean's open water zone extending from surface to floor?
Which ocean zone lies directly above the continental shelves?
What ecological role do most marine bacteria play in ocean ecosystems?
Which two gases are essential for photosynthesis in ocean water?
What is the primary international goal for ocean protection by 2030?
Which type of energy is generated from ocean waves and tides?
What percentage of Earth's biosphere is represented by ocean ecosystems?
Which element has the longest residence time in seawater?
What is an oceanic zone called that has extremely low oxygen levels?
Which visible marine apex predator is also known as an Orca?
What is the process by which photosynthetic organisms create organic matter from carbon dioxide in the ocean?
What is the term for the amount of dissolved salts in seawater?
What ocean zone is located between the low tide and high tide lines on a beach?
What is the term for the removal of too many fish from ocean waters, causing population decline?
Which ocean current encircles the continent of Antarctica?
What phenomenon causes ocean water to rise and fall due to the moon's gravitational pull?
What is the average temperature of deep ocean water?
Which ocean is the smallest by area and volume?
What ocean current is known for significantly influencing the climate of Western Europe?
What is the primary purpose of establishing marine protected areas in the ocean?
What term describes a dramatic change in salinity levels at different ocean depths?
What human activity is considered the primary cause of overfishing in modern oceans?
What is the major effect of overfishing on marine ecosystems?
What type of water from deep ocean regions gradually moves toward the equator as part of ocean circulation?
At what temperature does typical ocean water freeze?
What is the name of the deepest point in the ocean?
What type of microscopic organisms float freely in the ocean and form the base of most marine food chains?
What is the practice called when marine organisms are farmed for food production?
What is the average pH of ocean surface water?
What is the name of the longest mountain range on Earth?
What ocean process is essential for regulating global climate by transferring heat around the planet?
Which type of marine plant is commonly found in shallow coastal areas and provides habitat for many species?
What chemical process causes ocean acidification?
What is the primary gas that causes ocean acidification when absorbed by seawater?
Which marine habitat is known for its exceptional biodiversity and is often called the rainforest of the sea?
What is the average depth of the world's oceans?
Which international treaty establishes the legal framework for governing ocean rights and responsibilities?
choices: United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea

questions.q: Which group of animals includes crabs, lobsters, and shrimp found in oceans worldwide?
What is the residence time of sodium ions in ocean water, meaning how long they remain dissolved before being removed?
The Mariana Trench's deepest point, Challenger Deep, reaches a depth of approximately how many meters?
In which ocean basin does the Peru Current flow, creating one of the world's most productive fishing zones due to upwelling?
What is the primary mechanism by which phytoplankton in the mesopelagic zone produce energy despite receiving minimal sunlight?
The abyssal zone of the ocean begins at approximately what depth below the sea surface?
What geomorphological feature forms when a coral reef grows around a volcanic island that subsequently subsides beneath the ocean surface?
The El Niño Southern Oscillation originates from temperature anomalies in which ocean basin?
Which oceanic zone receives enough sunlight for photosynthesis to occur and typically extends to approximately 200 meters depth?
The Bay of Fundy experiences tidal ranges exceeding 16 meters due to which physical principle?
choices: Increased solar radiation during summer months
Resonance between tidal forcing and basin geometry
Coriolis effect interference with lunar pull
Unusual magnetic properties of the seafloor

questions.q: Which process describes the sinking of aggregated dead organic matter and fecal pellets to the ocean floor, serving as a carbon transport mechanism?
The Mid-Ocean Ridge system is primarily composed of which type of rock formed at divergent plate boundaries?
Which bioluminescent deep-sea organism uses counterillumination to mask its silhouette from predators below?
The Sargasso Sea, located within the North Atlantic, is characterized by which unique feature that distinguishes it from surrounding ocean areas?
choices: Absence of surface currents and accumulation of sargassum seaweed
Coldest water temperatures in the Atlantic

questions.q: Which whale species undertakes the longest annual migration of any mammal, traveling approximately 44,000 miles round-trip?
The mesopelagic zone, also called the twilight zone, corresponds to what depth range below the ocean surface?
Which oceanic phenomenon occurs when wind-driven surface water moves offshore, allowing nutrient-rich deep water to rise and replace it?
What is the name of the region in the ocean where the thermocline and halocline layers meet, creating a sharp density boundary?
Which ocean current, originating off the coast of Peru and Chile, brings cold water northward and significantly influences global climate patterns?
Which process, driven by the Coriolis effect, causes surface ocean water to flow at an angle to the direction of wind stress?
What is the primary mechanism by which atmospheric CO2 is converted to carbonic acid in seawater, leading to ocean acidification?
Which submarine topographic feature is characterized by steep-sided underwater canyons that channel sediment and nutrients from the continental shelf to the deep ocean?
What is the name of the luminescent phenomenon produced by certain marine organisms as a chemical reaction within their cells?
In what year did the Deepwater Horizon oil rig explosion release approximately 4.9 million barrels of crude oil into the Gulf of Mexico?
Which ocean nutrient, essential for phytoplankton growth but often depleted in tropical waters, is a limiting factor for primary productivity?
What is the technical term for the gradual submergence of landmasses due to the weight of accumulating sediments or the cooling of oceanic lithosphere?
Which microscopic, shell-bearing organisms form the primary component of most marine ooze deposits on the abyssal plain?
What is the name of the ocean ridge system that runs through the center of the Atlantic Ocean and marks the boundary between tectonic plates?
Which ocean zone receives less than 1% of surface light intensity and is inhabited primarily by chemosynthetic organisms?
What is the primary cause of the spring-neap tidal cycle that occurs approximately every two weeks?
choices: Fluctuations in the Earth's magnetic field
Alignment of the Sun and Moon relative to Earth

questions.q: Which measure of water density involves the calculation of salinity, temperature, and pressure combined into a single parameter?
Which hydrothermal vent ecosystem at the seafloor derives its energy from chemosynthetic bacteria rather than photosynthesis?
What is the primary limiting factor that prevents most ocean organisms from living in the hadal zone below 6,000 meters?
Which biogeographic realm encompasses ocean regions with high endemism rates and is heavily influenced by the Kuroshio Current and seasonal monsoons?
The Mariana Trench, the deepest known oceanic trench, is located in which ocean?
The continental shelf zone, characterized by shallow waters directly adjacent to continents, is also known by which scientific term?
What phenomenon occurs when ocean water absorbs excess atmospheric CO2, resulting in a decrease in pH?
The abyssal plains of the ocean floor exist at depths primarily between which two measurements?
The thermohaline circulation, a global ocean current system driven by differences in density, is primarily driven by variations in what?
Which zone of the ocean is defined as the region of open water beyond the continental shelf, including the deepest parts?
The Sargasso Sea, a region of the North Atlantic known for its unique ecosystem, is characterized by the presence of which floating seaweed?
At what approximate depth does the ocean's photic zone, where sunlight penetrates for photosynthesis, typically end?
The Challenger Deep, the lowest point in the world's oceans, reaches a depth of approximately how many meters?
The Antarctic Circumpolar Current, the world's largest ocean current by volume, flows in which direction?
Bioluminescent organisms in the ocean primarily use light production for which primary function?
The Mid-Ocean Ridge system, where new oceanic crust is formed, spans approximately how many kilometers globally?
Which ocean basin contains the majority of the world's hydrothermal vents and chemosynthetic ecosystems?
The euphotic zone's depth limitation is primarily determined by the penetration of which wavelength of light?
Which dissolved gas constitutes approximately 85% of the oxygen content in the world's oceans?
choices: Molecular oxygen diffused from the atmosphere

questions.q: In the ocean's water column, the halocline is primarily defined by a sharp gradient in which chemical property?
Oxygen minimum zones (OMZs) in the ocean occur naturally at intermediate depths in certain regions. What is the primary cause of these zones?
choices: Limited water mixing combined with high respiration by decomposing organic matter
Excessive photosynthesis by algae consuming all oxygen
High water temperature preventing oxygen dissolution

questions.q: The Sargasso Sea, a region of the North Atlantic Ocean, is unique because it is bounded by which major ocean current system rather than by land?
choices: The Gulf Stream and related Atlantic currents

questions.q: Coral bleaching occurs when corals expel their symbiotic zooxanthellae algae. What is the primary environmental trigger for this mass expulsion event?
choices: Prolonged exposure to elevated water temperatures
Excessive nutrient pollution from agricultural runoff
Increased levels of ocean acidification alone

questions.q: The abyssal zone of the ocean begins at approximately what depth below the surface?
Which process describes the upwelling of nutrient-rich deep ocean water to the surface, typically occurring along western continental margins?
At what depth does the twilight zone of the ocean, where sunlight becomes too dim for photosynthesis, typically end?
What is the primary mechanism by which the moon causes tides in Earth's oceans?
choices: Magnetic field interactions with salt water
Solar radiation heating certain ocean regions
Gravitational pull creating bulges in ocean water

questions.q: Which of the following is the primary source of dissolved oxygen in deep ocean water?
choices: Sinking of oxygen-rich surface water from polar regions
Chemical reactions between rocks and seawater

questions.q: Approximately what percentage of Earth's oxygen is produced by marine organisms?
What process describes the sinking of cold, dense water in polar regions that drives global ocean circulation?
In what year was the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea first signed, establishing international ocean governance?
Which marine habitat, found in shallow coastal waters, is one of the most productive ecosystems on Earth and serves as a nursery for many fish species?
What is the term for the region of an ocean where fresh water from rivers meets and mixes with salt water from the ocean?
Which of the following creatures is known to produce bioluminescence in the abyssal zone to attract prey?
What is the average salinity of ocean water, measured in parts per thousand (ppt)?
Approximately how much of the world's biodiversity is found in the ocean?
At what depth does the thermocline typically begin to form in tropical ocean waters?
What is the primary nutrient responsible for triggering harmful algal blooms in coastal ocean regions?
Which international initiative specifically aims to protect 30% of the world's oceans by the year 2030?
In which oceanic zone is sunlight still present but too dim to support most photosynthesis?
Which marine reptile must return to land exclusively to reproduce despite spending its entire adult life in the ocean?
What is the term for the continuous global body of saltwater that covers most of Earth's surface?
The Mid-Ocean Ridge system is notable for being which of the following?
choices: The longest mountain range on Earth

questions.q: According to FAO data from 2017, approximately what percentage of the world's fish stocks were classified as fully exploited or overfished?
Which location in the Pacific Ocean is known as the spacecraft cemetery due to the remote dumping of decommissioned satellites?
What is the primary source of marine plastic debris that accumulates in ocean gyres?
Which climate phenomenon, characterized by unusually warm ocean temperatures, is projected to increase in frequency as a result of global warming?
What is the name of the deepest oceanic trench, located in the western Pacific Ocean?
Which ocean current, originating near Japan, is one of the strongest and warmest currents in the world?
What percentage of Earth's oxygen is produced by oceanic phytoplankton?
Which human activity is the primary driver of increased ocean acidification since the Industrial Revolution?
Approximately what percentage of the world's ocean surface experiences dead zones with critically low oxygen levels?
At what depth does the photic zone typically end, where light intensity drops to approximately 1% of surface levels?
What is the process by which ocean water loses dissolved oxygen, often caused by warm water temperatures and stagnation?
In what year did the term 'Great Pacific Garbage Patch' first gain widespread scientific and public attention?
Which ocean current, flowing along the western coast of North America, contributes to a cooler climate in that region?
What type of marine structure, built by stony corals, supports some of the most biodiverse ecosystems on Earth?
The phenomenon where coral reefs lose their symbiotic algae and turn white in response to ocean stress is called what?
Which of the following is the primary source of excess nutrients that trigger harmful algal blooms in coastal ocean waters?
What term describes the enrichment of water bodies with excessive nutrients, leading to uncontrolled plant growth and low oxygen conditions?
In oceanography, what is the name of the sunlit upper layer of the ocean where photosynthesis can occur?
Roughly what percentage of Earth's surface is covered by ocean?
What is the largest animal ever known to have lived on Earth?

source.notes: Up to ~100 ft long and 200 tons.

questions.q: Approximately what percentage of Earth's water is salty/saline?
Which sea creature is widely considered the most intelligent invertebrate?

source.notes: Tool use, problem solving, mimicry.

questions.q: Which fish has both eyes on the same side of its body as an adult?

source.notes: Hatch with one eye on each side; one eye migrates as it matures.

questions.q: What sea mammal is sometimes nicknamed 'the sea cow'?

source.notes: Together with dugongs.

questions.q: Which marine reptile is the world's largest living reptile?

source.notes: Males can exceed 6 m and 1,000 kg.

questions.q: Which is the world's largest flatfish?

source.notes: Pacific halibut can reach over 8 ft and 500 lbs.

questions.q: Which Jovian moon is widely thought to harbor a salty subsurface ocean?

source.notes: Ganymede also has subsurface water but Europa is the prime astrobiology target.

questions.q: Which Saturnian moon is famous for ice plumes erupting from its south pole, suggesting a subsurface ocean?

source.notes: Plumes discovered by Cassini in 2005.

questions.q: Which large coral reef system off Australia is the world's largest living structure?

source.notes: Over 2,300 km long.

questions.q: What is the term for cyclic wind- and current-driven warm/cool fluctuations in the equatorial Pacific?

source.notes: El Niño = warm phase, La Niña = cool phase.

questions.q: What is the term for the sinking of carbon-rich organic material from the surface to the deep ocean?

source.notes: Crucial process for ocean carbon sequestration.

questions.q: What tiny shrimp-like crustacean is a primary food source for baleen whales?

source.notes: Antarctic krill (Euphausia superba) is the most abundant by biomass.

questions.q: Which sea turtle, the largest in the world, can dive deeper than 1,000 m?

source.notes: Leatherback can exceed 7 ft long and 2,000 lbs.

questions.q: The scientific study of the oceans is called what?

source.notes: A branch of Earth science. Includes physical, chemical, biological, and geological oceanography.

questions.q: Which ocean was designated as the fifth ocean by the IHO in 2000 (though disputed)?

source.notes: Surrounds Antarctica. National Geographic officially recognized it as the 5th ocean in 2021.

questions.q: What type of sea creature is a whale shark?

source.notes: Despite the name, it's the largest fish species, not a whale. Filter feeder.

questions.q: Coral reefs are primarily made of what?

source.notes: Formed by colonies of tiny animals called coral polyps secreting calcium carbonate.

questions.q: Which seagoing creatures use echolocation to navigate and find food?

source.notes: Send out clicks and listen to echoes. Bats also use echolocation.

questions.q: Why does the ocean appear blue?

source.notes: Water absorbs red wavelengths and scatters blue. Sky reflection adds to the effect.

questions.q: What is the body of water separating the UK from continental Europe called?

source.original_a: Gulf Stream in the Atlantic Ocean
source.notes: Narrowest point: ~34 km (21 miles) at the Strait of Dover. Chunnel runs under it.

questions.q: Which saltwater body is famous for being 9 times saltier than the ocean, letting swimmers float?

source.notes: Technically a lake, not a sea. ~34% salinity vs ocean's 3.5%.

questions.q: What is an oceanic tsunami primarily caused by?

source.notes: Also caused by volcanic eruptions or underwater landslides. Can travel 500+ mph.

questions.q: What is the process of removing salt from seawater to make it drinkable?
Which ocean surrounds the continent of Antarctica?
Which strait connects the Pacific Ocean to the Arctic Ocean?
Which is the largest species of shark?
What is an underwater mountain called?
Which animal forms a symbiotic relationship with sea anemones?
What is the deepest part of the ocean called?
Which of these is a type of marine mammal?
Which famous researcher used the vessel Calypso for underwater exploration?
How many oceans are recognized by most geographic organizations?
How many arms does a typical octopus have?
Which of these fish is known as the fastest swimmer in the ocean?
Which marine mammal is known for performing acrobatic jumps and spins?
Which animal has the ability to change its gender during its lifetime?
In which ocean is the Great Barrier Reef located?
How many chambers does a nautilus shell have?
Which marine animal is the closest living relative to whales?
Which jellyfish species is known for its extremely long trailing tentacles and venomous sting?
What is the name of the supercontinent that existed millions of years ago and contained all Earth's landmasses?
What type of fish is a seahorse?
Which of these creatures lives in the deepest parts of the ocean?
What do corals primarily feed on?
Which of these animals is known for producing bioluminescence in the ocean?
In what year did the RMS Titanic sink in the North Atlantic?
Which sea creature has a shell made of nacre, also called mother-of-pearl?
What do seahorses use their prehensile tail for?
What is the primary cause of ocean currents?
Which animal can live the longest among sea turtles?
What is a group of sea otters called?
Which fish is famous for its ability to change color and texture to match its surroundings?
What type of animal is the clownfish commonly found living alongside in coral reefs?
Which species of whale can dive deeper than 7,000 feet in pursuit of giant squid?
The Mid-Ocean Ridge system is primarily formed by what geological process?
choices: Seafloor spreading at divergent plate boundaries

questions.q: What is the primary cause of bioluminescence in deep-sea organisms?
choices: Chemical reactions involving luciferin and luciferase enzymes
Reflection of sunlight from specialized scales

questions.q: Which marine animal possesses a distributed nervous system with no centralized brain?
The Sargasso Sea is characterized by which unique oceanographic feature?
choices: High concentration of sargassum seaweed and low nutrient levels

questions.q: What term describes the explosive dispersal of gametes by corals during synchronized spawning events?
The term 'dead zone' in oceanography refers to areas with what characteristic?
choices: Regions with extreme water temperature fluctuations
Hypoxic or anoxic conditions with insufficient oxygen to support most life

questions.q: Which marine mammal can remain submerged for up to 2 hours while hunting?
What is the Ekman spiral phenomenon primarily used to explain?
choices: The spiral shell formation in nautiluses
How wind-driven ocean currents change direction with depth
Tidal bore formation in river estuaries
The rotation of whale migration patterns

questions.q: The chemosynthetic bacteria found near hydrothermal vents derive energy from which source?
choices: Oxidation of hydrogen sulfide and other inorganic compounds

questions.q: Which cephalopod species can regenerate lost limbs but exhibits asymmetrical regeneration patterns?
What is the primary function of the counter-shading coloration pattern found in many pelagic fish?
choices: Camouflage in the water column by matching ambient light conditions

questions.q: The term 'osmoregulation' in marine fish primarily addresses which physiological challenge?
choices: Maintaining stable body temperature in varying ocean temperatures
Controlling buoyancy through gas bladder adjustment
Balancing salt and water levels in hypersaline or hypotonic environments

questions.q: What is the primary mechanism by which sea level changes over decadal timescales due to climate factors?
choices: Thermal expansion of seawater and melting of glaciers and ice sheets
Changes in the Earth's rotational axis

questions.q: The cnidocytes found in sea anemones and corals function primarily to accomplish what?
choices: Capture and paralyze prey through nematocyst discharge
Regulate water salinity in the organism's tissues

questions.q: Which marine mammal possesses the longest migration route of any animal on Earth, traveling approximately 44,000 miles annually?
Which species of deep-sea shark is characterized by a flattened head and electroreceptive organs that detect electrical fields generated by prey?
What biological phenomenon describes the vertical migration of marine organisms between deep and shallow waters on a daily cycle?
What is the scientific term for the zone of the ocean where light penetration becomes insufficient for photosynthesis, typically occurring below 200 meters?
What geochemical process, driven by chemosynthetic bacteria, occurs at hydrothermal vents and serves as the primary energy source for vent communities?
Which parasitic copepod attaches to the eyes and brain tissue of the Greenland shark, potentially causing blindness in the host?
What is the primary mechanism by which the Portuguese man o' war, despite being commonly called a jellyfish, maintains its colonial structure and buoyancy?
choices: A specialized float called a pneumatophore

questions.q: What specialized structure found in many bony fishes allows them to regulate buoyancy by controlling gas exchange with surrounding water?
What is the name of the symbiotic relationship between clownfish and sea anemones, where the fish receives protection in exchange for providing nutrients and parasite removal?
Which deep-sea fish species exhibits extreme sexual dimorphism, with males permanently fusing to females through a process called sexual parasitism?
Which endangered marine reptile, found primarily in the Indo-Pacific region, is the only living member of the family Dermochelyidae?
Which species of lanternfish, distinguished by photophores arranged in specific patterns, comprises the largest biomass of any fish species in the mesopelagic zone?
What is the name of the acoustic phenomenon used by sperm whales to stun and disorient their deep-diving prey, believed to be generated through specialized nasal structures?
Which deep-sea hydrothermal vent mineral precipitate creates towering structures and is composed primarily of iron sulfides?
Which marine mammal species possesses a counterclockwise-spiraling tusk that can grow up to 3 meters in length?
What is the name of the bioluminescent dinoflagellate species commonly responsible for red tides in coastal waters?
Which ocean basin contains the Tonga Trench and is located in the western Pacific region?
Which deep-sea fish species is notable for possessing a hinged skull and an expandable stomach to swallow prey larger than itself?
Which cephalopod species is capable of rapid chromatophore changes and possesses three hearts and blue copper-based blood?
What is the name of the abyssal plain in the Atlantic Ocean that contains the Challenger Deep, the deepest ocean trench?
choices: No such plain exists in the Atlantic

questions.q: Which process describes the decomposition of organic matter in anoxic ocean sediments, producing hydrogen sulfide and methane?
What is the approximate age of the oldest ocean floor, located near subduction zones at continental margins?
Which marine reptile species has a specialized salt gland that allows it to excrete excess salt from seawater consumption?
What is the technical name for the symbiotic relationship between coral polyps and zooxanthellae algae that enables reef survival?
Which oceanographic instrument measures the vertical profile of water density by detecting changes in electrical conductivity and temperature?
What phenomenon occurs when stratified ocean layers prevent vertical mixing, creating isolated water masses with distinct temperature and salinity properties?
Which shark species possesses rostral (saw-like) tooth structures on its snout and is classified in the Pristidae family?
What is the primary mechanism by which the thermocline layer prevents nutrient-rich deep water from mixing with surface waters in stratified oceans?
Which ocean current brings warm water from the Gulf of Mexico northeastward along the eastern coast of North America?
What is the maximum depth of the Mariana Trench, the deepest part of the world's oceans?
Which marine mammal is known to produce the loudest calls of any animal on Earth?
What adaptation allows the anglerfish to attract prey in the deep ocean?
Which species of sea turtle is named for the color of its fat rather than its shell?
Which coral structure forms a ring-shaped atoll around a central lagoon?
Which large marine reptile is known for its ability to hold its breath for over 90 minutes while diving?
What structural feature allows the remora fish to attach itself to larger marine animals?
Which chemical compound, produced by some marine organisms, creates a glow in the dark phenomenon?
Which explorer is credited with being the first European to reach the Indian Ocean by sailing around Africa?
What is the name of the ocean current that flows along the eastern coast of South America and significantly influences the climate of southern Brazil?
Which marine mammal is the only cetacean species that primarily hunts cooperatively to create 'bubble nets' to trap fish?
Which sea turtle species is named for the leathery texture of its shell rather than a bony carapace?
What is the primary function of the swim bladder in most fish species?
Which marine plant, found in kelp forests, can grow up to two feet per day under ideal conditions?
What is the term for the process by which marine organisms' shells and skeletons accumulate on the ocean floor over millions of years?
Which whale species is famous for its haunting, complex songs that can travel hundreds of miles underwater?
Which species of shark is the largest filter feeder in the ocean, consuming only plankton?
Which marine sanctuary, established as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, is known for its exceptional biodiversity in Southeast Asia?
Which crustacean species is known for its ability to regenerate lost limbs and can live for over 100 years?
What is the primary reason that the Mediterranean Sea has higher salinity than the Atlantic Ocean?
Which octopus species is the most venomous marine animal known to science, despite its small size?
In marine biology, what term describes an organism that drifts passively with ocean currents rather than swimming actively?
What is the name of the deep oceanic zone that begins at approximately 1,000 meters below the surface and is characterized by complete darkness?
Which ocean current is responsible for moderating the climate of Western Europe and is an extension of the Gulf Stream?
What is the primary type of skeleton found in cartilaginous fish such as sharks and rays?
Which process describes the accumulation of toxic substances in organisms at higher levels of the food chain?
Which bioluminescent organism is responsible for creating the phenomenon known as 'red tide' when it blooms in large numbers?
What is the scientific term for the symbiotic relationship between coral polyps and the zooxanthellae algae living within their tissues?
What is the term for the rate at which seawater becomes less dense moving from the surface downward due to temperature change?
Which deep-sea creature possesses eyes that can rotate independently and has no stomach?
Which crustacean is known for producing a cavitation bubble that collapses violently to stun or kill prey?
What is the name of the tidal phenomenon that occurs in certain river estuaries where a large wave of water rushes inland against the river current?
Which marine invertebrate can turn itself inside-out as a defense mechanism and later regenerate its lost internal organs?
What is the primary nutrient that stimulates harmful algal blooms when it enters coastal marine ecosystems in excess?
Which whalebone whale species has the most asymmetrical skull in the animal kingdom, with one ear being larger than the other?
Which sea creature can change color and skin texture to camouflage?

source.notes: Uses chromatophores in skin cells.

questions.q: In which species of fish does the male carry and give birth to the young?

source.notes: Female deposits eggs into male's brood pouch.

questions.q: Which sea creature can regenerate lost limbs?

source.notes: Some species can regrow an entire body from a single arm.

questions.q: Which floating marine creature isn't a true jellyfish, but a colony of organisms with venomous tentacles?

source.notes: A siphonophore — colony of zooids.

questions.q: Which strait separates Asia and North America?

source.notes: ~53 miles wide between Russia & Alaska.

questions.q: What's the world's largest island?

source.notes: Australia is a continental landmass, not classified as an island.

questions.q: What's the smallest porpoise/whale species — critically endangered, found in Mexico's Gulf of California?

source.notes: Fewer than 20 left in the wild as of 2024.

questions.q: Which large gulf is located between Iran and the Arabian Peninsula?
What type of water body supports marine life and has high salt content?
What is the most effective way to prevent overfishing in the world's oceans?
Which organization is responsible for maritime safety and marine pollution regulation?
What is the primary goal of marine conservation efforts?
What marine resource is mined from the ocean floor for energy production?
What is the term for the depth at which light intensity is only 1% of the surface value?
Which oceanic phenomenon is responsible for major climate impacts in coastal regions?
What percentage of the Earth's water is contained in its oceans?
